title: FireFusion
summary: ConvFormer-based spatiotemporal model for forecasting wildfire ignition and cause in Washington State.
subtitle: A multi-modal spatiotemporal deep learning framework that integrates meteorology, fuels, topography, and human-pressure signals to forecast next-day wildfire ignition probability and ignition cause across Washington State.

cardImage.alt: Predicted next-day wildfire ignition probability map over Washington State

heroImage.alt: Predicted next-day wildfire ignition probability map over Washington State, with elevated risk concentrated along the eastern Cascades

sections.title: Overview
sections.body:
Wildfire ignition is one of the harder problems in physics-aware modeling: ignitions are extremely rare relative to the number of candidate pixels and days, and roughly **85–90% of recent Washington wildfires are human-caused**, which means meteorology and fuels alone aren't enough to explain where fires start. *FireFusion* tackles this with a ConvFormer-based spatiotemporal architecture that fuses meteorological, topographic, vegetation, and human-pressure signals into a unified next-day ignition forecast.

The model jointly predicts (i) the probability of a new ignition at every grid cell on day `T+1`, and (ii) the most likely ignition cause class (lightning, human, industrial, debris/burning), trained end-to-end with physics-aware masking that excludes water, non-burnable terrain, and currently-burning cells from the loss.

This was a personal research project completed during my MS in AI at UT Austin.

sections.title: Key contributions
sections.intro: FireFusion makes four contributions targeting the gaps in existing wildfire ignition models - most of which lean on either static fire-weather indices or low-modality CNN baselines.
items: **ConvFormer spatiotemporal backbone.** A convolutional encoder followed by windowed spatial attention, channel-mixing attention, and temporal attention - explicitly modeling space, cross-feature interaction, and multi-day temporal evolution as separate axes.
**Bi-head decoder.** Ignition probability and ignition cause are predicted on the same fine-resolution grid as a coupled multi-class problem, rather than collapsing to a single hazard score.

**Physics-aware masking.** Water, non-burnable terrain, missing-data regions, and active fires are masked out of the loss and output space, preventing the model from learning non-causal correlations or treating ongoing fires as repeated ignitions.

**Multi-modal learned representations.** Domain-engineered features (Fosberg FWI, multi-day precipitation, decomposed wind/aspect vectors, distance-to-WUI, KDE priors) are mixed with raw inputs and learned shared representations across feature classes - rather than relying on either raw rasters or hand-crafted indices alone.

sections.title: Study area
sections.alt: Topographic map of Washington State showing elevation and major roadways
sections.caption: Washington State features a strong east-west polarity in climate, fuels, and human pressure - making it a hotbed for dynamic fire-weather modeling. Source: YellowMaps.

sections.title: Feature engineering
sections.body:
Rather than feeding raw gridded products directly into the model, FireFusion builds a compact, derived feature set organized around five categories: **fuels and vegetation, weather and climate, topography, human presence, and historical fire activity**. Inputs are organized as a unified spatiotemporal datacube with explicit `(time, y, x)` coordinates so that all sources - point ignitions, vector perimeters, and gridded rasters - can be masked, interpolated, and stacked through a shared pipeline.

The acquisition pipeline runs five steps per source: *aggregation* (clip to study area), *reprojection* (equal-area, nearest-neighbor for categoricals), *time interpolation* to a daily index, *derivation* of features like rolling precipitation, distance-to-road, and decomposed wind/aspect vectors, and *normalization* (z-score with `log1p` for long-tail variables like population density). Sinusoidal features are decomposed into component vectors so the model never has to learn the 360°→0° wrap.

sections.title: Data sources
sections.alt: Table summarizing the ten geospatial data sources used by FireFusion, including NLCD, gridMET, NASA GPW, MODIS products, USDA WUI, USFS occurrence and perimeter layers, and Census TIGER/Line roads
sections.caption: Ten complementary sources span land cover, daily meteorology, vegetation indices, population, wildland–urban interface, road networks, and multi-source fire labels.

sections.title: Labels and masking
sections.body:
The ignition label for day `T+1` is defined as a **transition event** - a cell labeled positive only if it is not burning at `T` and is burning at `T+1`. Cells already on fire at `T` are masked from the loss entirely. This framing is critical: it forces the model to predict the *onset* of fire rather than its continued spread, and it guarantees that reported ignition probabilities at inference are confined to physically plausible, currently-burnable landscapes.

Cause labels are defined only on newly-ignited pixels and mapped to four classes (lightning, human, industrial, debris/burning), with ambiguous codes routed to an *unknown* bucket that is excluded from the cause head's loss.

sections.title: Architecture
sections.body:
FireFusion processes inputs of shape `(B, T, C, H, W)` through four stages before a two-head decoder:

**1. Spatial Encoder.** A residual CNN (ResNet-style stem + two downsampling stages) compresses each daily grid to `(B, T, embed_dim, H', W')`, expanding the receptive field for downstream attention while preserving fine-scale geography through skip connections.

**2. Windowed Spatial Attention.** Each `(H', W')` grid is partitioned into `P x P` non-overlapping windows, with multi-head self-attention applied within each window. Window size is chosen to match characteristic topographic scales - keeping complexity roughly linear in the number of pixels rather than quadratic.

**3. Channel-Mixing Attention.** For each spatial-temporal location, feature channels are treated as tokens and attended over. This is where the model explicitly learns synergy between, say, dry-fuel signals and wind exposure - the kind of cross-feature interaction that pixel-CNN baselines tend to miss.

**4. Temporal Attention.** Applied *last*, so that order and identity of individual days are preserved. The model can attend across the full lookback window, learning multi-day drying sequences, wind buildup, and longer-horizon population pressure.

**5. Bi-head decoder.** A shared upsampling path feeds two `1x1` conv heads - one for ignition probability, one for the four-way cause distribution - both at full resolution.

sections.title: Training approach
sections.body:
Training had to reconcile three pressures: extreme class imbalance, the need to learn from temporally-coherent sequences rather than isolated snapshots, and a multi-task objective. Sliding spatiotemporal windows of `T` past days are sampled with stride, and windows whose target day contains at least one ignition are **oversampled**; the BCE loss additionally carries a positive weight on the order of **6,000:1** to prevent collapse to a trivial 'no fire' prediction.

Optimization uses AdamW with a cosine-annealed schedule and warmup, gradient clipping for outlier robustness, and PR-AUC on a held-out validation set as the early-stopping criterion - chosen over ROC because the dataset is dominated by negatives and ROC is misleading at this prevalence.

sections.title: Results - ignition head
sections.body:
On the held-out test set, FireFusion achieves an **AUPRC of ~0.62**, against a no-skill baseline of **~0.08** set by the empirical positive rate. That ~8x gap means the model concentrates a large fraction of true ignitions into a small fraction of high-scoring cells.

At the F1-optimal threshold, the binary confusion matrix shows the model correctly rejecting the overwhelming majority of non-ignition cells while recovering a substantial fraction of true ignitions. Most errors are false positives - a deliberate consequence of choosing a threshold that prioritizes recall, since missing an event costs far more than over-flagging in an early-warning context.

image.alt: Precision-recall curve for FireFusion vs. no-skill baseline; FireFusion AUPRC = 0.62, baseline = 0.08

sections.title: Results - cause head
sections.alt: Multi-class confusion matrix for ignition cause prediction across no-ignition, lightning, human, and other classes
sections.caption: Cause-head confusion matrix evaluated only on cells with valid ignition labels. Most error mass falls on the lightning/human boundary - cases where convective activity and human exposure overlap - and on the heterogeneous 'other' class.

sections.title: Spatial probability map
sections.alt: Predicted next-day ignition probability map for a test day over Washington, with elevated risk along the eastern slopes of the Cascades and selected interior basins
sections.caption: Predicted next-day ignition probability for a test day. Risk concentrates along the Puget Sound corridor, the eastern slopes of the Cascades, and selected interior basins - where fuel availability, human access, and dry-lightning regimes overlap. Water bodies, glaciated terrain, and irrigated valleys are correctly suppressed.

sections.title: Takeaways and limitations
sections.intro: The qualitative behavior is consistent with known fire climatology: the model assigns elevated probabilities along convective bands and high-elevation ridges while suppressing urban cores and irrigated valleys. Conditioning experiments - same instantaneous temperature and humidity, different antecedent precipitation - confirm the temporal attention block is genuinely using multi-day context rather than collapsing to same-day conditions.
items: **Resolution ceiling.** All experiments use a 2 km grid. Many fine-scale ignition drivers (fuel breaks, narrow canyons, small road networks) operate at tens of meters - but attention scales quadratically in window cell count, and the project trained on a single RTX 3070, which forces a real trade-off between resolution and training stability.

**Latency-affected inputs.** Vegetation composites (NDVI, LAI) arrive on multi-day cadences and fire history layers lag by weeks to months. Even with temporal interpolation and forward-fill, inputs reflect a smoothed view of the landscape - a likely source of systematic bias.

**Single-region training.** Learned relationships reflect Washington-specific fuels, climate regimes, and reporting practices. The framework should generalize, but cross-region transfer would require retraining and careful validation.

**What worked.** Decomposing sinusoidal features (wind, aspect, day-of-year) into component vectors materially helped - it removed the 360°→0° wrap as something the model had to learn. Treating ignition as a *transition* event and masking active fires from the loss was the single most important framing decision.
